title: Congratulations, You've Won!
introduction: Sam just received this text. Tap anything suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text:
🎉 CONGRATULATIONS! You've been selected as a WINNER in our monthly drawing! You've won a €500 Amazon gift card!

Claim your prize: http://amaz0n-prize-claim.tk/winner?ref=82647
script.explanation: You can't win a competition you didn't enter. The URL uses a zero instead of an 'o' in Amazon, and ends in .tk — a free domain used almost exclusively for scams. Amazon's real domain is amazon.co.uk or amazon.com — nothing else.

script.text: This promotion is part of our annual customer appreciation campaign.
I didn't enter any competition... but €500 would be nice. Let me check the link.

PRIZE CLAIM SITE:
'To verify your identity and release your €500 gift card, please enter your full name, home address, date of birth, and card details for the €2.99 delivery fee.'
script.flag_label: Personal info + payment for a prize
script.explanation: Two red flags in one: no real prize requires your card details to claim it, and a €2.99 'delivery fee' is a direct money grab. The personal information (name, address, DOB) is being harvested for identity fraud regardless of whether you pay.

script.text: We have sent prize notifications to customers across the region.
I'll just pay the small fee — it's only €2.99 and I could get €500 back.

Payment processed. Your gift card will arrive within 14 working days.
[No gift card ever arrives. Sam's card details are now in the hands of scammers.]
script.explanation: The prize was never real. The €2.99 was the actual goal — multiplied across thousands of victims. Sam's card details can now be used for further fraud. The 'gift card in 14 days' delay is to give scammers time before the victim realises.

learning_objectives: You cannot win a competition you didn't enter
Check URLs carefully — scammers use look-alike domains
No legitimate prize requires payment or card details to claim
Small fees are direct theft, not delivery costs
Report prize scam texts to 7726 (spam reporting number in UK/Ireland)
debrief:
Prize scam texts are sent in bulk to millions of numbers. The scammers don't know your name — the prize is fake and the text is identical for everyone who receives it.

**The tell:** You can't win something you didn't enter. Full stop.

**The real goal** is either your card details (for fraud), your personal data (for identity theft), or the small 'fee' — often just enough that victims don't bother reporting it.

**What to do:** Delete the text. If you want to report it, forward it to 7726 — the free spam SMS reporting service in both UK and Ireland.
